speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- your head-- were the contents of the head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then by chance, through Mrs. Pell, I met Mr. Mitchell. I don't know how I met him, actually, but I got to know him. He was head of the intelligence service. And Commander [INAUDIBLE], Richard [INAUDIBLE], you know, who sank boats in the [INAUDIBLE] Canal before, quite well-known. He was married to Betty Chester, one of the Co-Optimists. And I got to know these people-- Donald Darling in the British intelligence-- and I was hanging out with them a little bit. And then finally, we get these boats and go to-- to England, and with Bill Holland and this man, Eddie [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about having to leave France in that time? Because you must have been quite caught up with everything that was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- you know, I was relieved, but I was sort of-- there was a point there where I felt why didn't I stay and do something else? Find another way, you know. But I couldn't have, of course, because I was too much on the list. And you know, they'd photographed me and everything. But I was thinking, why couldn't I find something else to do? But then I was so anxious now for the RAF thinking, well, that's the best thing I can do. I certainly can't do it anymore here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for you to join the RAF?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really. As I say, I was probably already quite ill, but my physical was quite all right. And I had flown before, so that was-- that part was all right. But then I went to a private doctor, and I had blood in my oxygen mask one time, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when you were flying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I went to a private doctor, and he reported me because I had on an RAF uniform, of course, and I had here, instead of USA, I had Virginia. A lot of people thought it was the Virgin Islands, but it was-- and when I was in the Foreign Legion, [INAUDIBLE] was a 12-year-old sister of my closest friend, Phillip [INAUDIBLE], and she used to wear my [FRENCH], you know, from the Legion. They would say, what is that? And she'd say, oh, it's a secret society I belong to.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this doctor-- naturally, I was the only person who had Virginia on it because you weren't supposed to have. I'd had it embroidered, and he reported it and found out where I was, and they said that I was contagious. And our commanding officer took me to dinner, and he sat there, and he said, what the hell were you trying to do, infect everybody here? So how long were you in the RAF for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I guess eight months. Seven or eight months, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during your time in the RAF, what were you doing? Flying to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flying, but then I-- then when I broke down-- I broke down and all-- they sent me to-- first to a hospital in England, and then eventually to Canada. And then I faked my way back into America. I crossed-- I bet you I'm the only person who ever crossed into America without one paper, without-- only with a bus ticket. But I did it exactly-- I knew exactly how to do it because of the experiences. I bet I'm the only one who ever did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not have any papers with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just didn't, because I left the hospital without permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sneaked out one night, and I tell you, after I got about 25 meters away, I was wishing I was back in that bed, I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you actually in the hospital for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about a year all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: A long time to recover.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that during that time, you met up with your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he came up to see me. He read in a newspaper about it, and so he came to see me. And the British published something about it, and you know, they were-- at that time, the British were looking for an excuse to publicize an American in the RAF, so I got a lot of publicity that I didn't-- I wonder who are they talking about? A lot of the-- a lot of newspaper articles [INAUDIBLE] I think, I wonder who they're thinking, who they're talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they published articles about you being in the Air Force, did they know anything about your activities in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the slightest idea. Not the slightest idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So had they have known, they would have had a lot to write about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but you know, once it was over, it was just a memory, and it was for us. You know, we never thought about-- as I say, none of my friends knew this, including my wife. But when this Holocaust museum opened, I mean, a lot of people [INAUDIBLE]. I have a lot of Jewish friends being in the film business and all, and-- and you can't believe what some of the things they-- they say and some of the things they've written about me since, and all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got back into America through the back door.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about I guess 19 then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were still very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, maybe 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got back into America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I heard about this Dr. Gilbert and Webb, doctors who had invented a very revolutionary-- pneumothorax, but a special one, you know. And it meant that you could get back into action even with tuberculosis, which closes the lung down, which I did. And-- and it actually saved my life, of course. And so I went to Colorado Springs where they were, and stayed in the hospital there for a while, and then again, I left, and then eventually got back in an ambulance corps and went back to Europe. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never went home at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The American Field Service. I went back with them. I had somebody, Bill Holland, my friend, who was killed. His sister came with me, and we went down to the-- to the Seaman's Institute, and we found a seaman who was not anxious to go to war anymore because they were sinking ships everywhere. And he took my examination with the-- with the Field Service, and we exchanged papers. And he used my name, and I used his, and then with that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a seaman, just some jerk tried to blackmail us and everything. He did everything he could to get more money after the [INAUDIBLE]. But then the way--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you used his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the way--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to stick with that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to stick with that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I used-- I used his name just to take the examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. You swapped names before, so you went as--

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, he went in with my name and passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Passed, and you went with his.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in with his. And how we prevented him from blackmailing us for more money, I said, well, now you've got 4F on your-- your record, and you will never have to go to sea again. So that's it. He said, oh, yes, yes, all right, here's the paper, and that worked fine. And then I went back. And then CB Alexander, who was the head of our unit, he was worried about my coughing and all like that. And it wasn't a good idea, you know, being in so close contact with so many, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So many people.

speaker: interviewee
text: These other people

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the States, you went back to Europe, and where did you go to in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been to Vosges Mountains, and then I joined the Foreign Legion. And then the day the war was over, I-- I'm the only person who ever resigned from the Foreign Legion. I said to my commanding officer, I'm not feeling too well. Maybe I'd better go see a doctor. He said, oh, come off it, [INAUDIBLE], we know all about it. They had-- I had disappeared to go and take my pneumothorax refill, and they thought maybe I was a spy. Because it was when the Battle Of The Bulge, you know, the Americans came over in our section and all, and they thought, well. So they checked me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew who you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they never--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Vosges Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed there the whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris. No, in Paris. Then I went to Paris. I went to the hospital again.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when it finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day it finished, in the Vosges Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd been there all that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A while, just a short while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing-- what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infantry. Nothing much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And-- and what did you see around you, you know, during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Alsatians, you know, a lot of people thought because they are part German anyway, you know, but they were quite aggressive against the Germans. They got a very bad press, I think, because I think the Alsatians were much maligned during the-- during the war. People thought, well, maybe they-- and Colmar, for example, was a big, big center for the Nazis, and it was one of the worst fightings of the war. There was a funny story about one of the-- about something in Colmar. There was a young kid, you know, very young, and his job was to go out and scrape the ice out from under the cars and all that, the command cars and the jeeps, of the German equivalent of the jeep. And he was a businessman, too, so he would take the antifreeze, drain it out, and sell it, but he'd put water in in place of it. And of course, when the machines would take off and then stop for an hour, it would freeze up. And I suppose this man did-- this kid-- did more to frustrate the German army than--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then anybody else. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: there were some terribly funny things that happened during the war. Not so many funny things in Marseille at that time, but when one did, it was so hilarious that it got around. And you know, some of these people knowing that they were right on the verge of death anyway, of being caught, had a surprising sense of humor, some of them. I remember one or two. Klaus Dohrn was one of them. Konrad Heiden. I didn't know who these people were. I had no idea, you know. They were just-- just numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So the war ended, and then you went to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back to hospital and stayed quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was still in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, back in the States then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- where was the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first place that I went in was back in France, the first hospital. Then I didn't have any money, and I had TB, and it had it on my discharge, pulmonary tuberculosis, I think in red ink if I'm not mistaken. And so nobody-- I remember a woman, when I was applying for a job, opened the window when I left to shoo the germs out. And I was painting signs in the [INAUDIBLE] to make a living, and I knew a lot of people in France, in Paris, but I didn't want to look like-- I looked like death warmed over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I ran into a man, a journalist. That story I told you about, the little boy? That was the first story I told him. He wanted to know-- he saw me, what was left over of a Legion uniform, and he wanted to know why and all that. And to mislead him, I told him stories about people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I spent a few nights, till late at night, sitting in a bar telling him all these various stories about people I'd known during the war, and getting a lot of money for it, and eating awfully well, and sleeping in the Hotel [INAUDIBLE]. And then I went to Spain where it was warm and all, and didn't have to pay very much. I got much better then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came back-- and then one day, I was walking in the-- I went in Fouquet's on the Champs Elysees really and truly just out of old time's sake. By now, I still looked pretty awful. But I ran into an old friend of mine, [? Alan Bernheim, ?] who had known me. We'd known each other in Marseille. He had helped us a little bit, you know, and was a very interesting man. Had a fascinating mother and all. And he said, oh, I know somebody making a film about Marseille, and you could probably get a job as technical advisor on the [INAUDIBLE] there. So I went to Jean [INAUDIBLE], who was making this film, and sure enough, he hired me. And then one day we were filming on a tramp steamer that they had rented just for the day, because it had to leave for a run that night. And the sun was going down on the buildings, and the higher paid help, the director, and the producer, and the camera man, and everybody over in a corner, worried. It seemed that the actor they had brought down to play the captain of the ship was on their way to the hospital to have his appendix out, and they had to establish the fact that they were in that section of Marseille.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- the director had the captain's hat in his hand. Tried it on everybody. It fitted me. He said, you stand over by the rail, and you say so-and-so when Jean [INAUDIBLE] comes. Anyway, by then, Jean [INAUDIBLE] and I were writing the dialog anyway, so I knew what to say. And when we get back to Paris, we'll get a real actor to do the close ups and the rest of the role. So I did what I thought you were supposed to do, and I heard 'cut and print it.' And they moved the camera in close, and so on, and I had the role. Got back to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next film I did was the second lead in the big film of the year, and I went on making one film after the other. Still didn't know where the camera was the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you went back to America after--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to go back, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several years before I went back. And I went back-- I'm trying to remember why. I really don't remember why. I didn't go back to my home. I hadn't been back to my home town yet, but I will one day because I have a big affection for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- so you were just in the film industry for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Making films.

speaker: interviewee
text: All over the world, 31 countries. It was a sort of a joke among people who know me. They say, give me a one way ticket, and I'll take any job without reading the script. But I have some good roles. Sophia Loren. I had a lead with her once, and with Dyan Cannon, another woman, and May Britt in the big film of the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said at one point that-- you said to me the pre-interview that you married. Where did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well then, after that, when I was in Paris during this time, you know, I was working in the theater at that time. I'd already made several films, including where I was Athos. And I was on the-- for some reason, I don't know why, they wanted me to dub-- you know what dubbing is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted me to direct the dubbing of the film, a Canadian company, but it was a French film with [INAUDIBLE] and I've forgotten who else. And so I asked them, I said, what's dubbing? I should have told them, shouldn't I? But anyway, they hired me, and I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had an old Citroen at that time, but I would always take the bus so I could work on the dialogue while I was going back and forth to the studio. And I ran into a-- there was a famous painter from Romania named Stoenescu, and his son, Dan Stoenescu, was living in Paris. And he said, I'm going to have dinner with the most glamorous woman in Paris. You want to come with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, I can't. I've got to get up in the morning early. And it was when the buses, you know, were open on the back. And it was raining, and I saw him passing. And we stopped for a red light, and I saw him passing to go to see this lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, what the hell? And just as the bus pulled out, I jumped off and went with him. And then eventually, we married. And here it is, she is a-- she was a German, and here my feeling about the Germans were so incredible. But her mother had been very badly treated by the Nazis because she wouldn't stand up when-- she was a little girl then-- wouldn't stand up when Hitler came into the room. And so they knew what the family's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Fritz Thyssen was her uncle, who had been put in a concentration camp, and you'll notice his name is on our list. He was the only Gentile on our list. We know-- I had no idea that she was related to him. None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met him through-- you met her through this friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through this other friend. But much, much later she read in a book about Fritz Thyssen being smuggled out by the us, and needless to say that made me very-- and now I have a daughter with seven children, and one of them is on the world champion polo team. I think he's 18 years old or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now just going back to your first wife, where did you marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was working in film, so I was away all the time, but she lived in Paris. And when my daughter was five years old, she was in an automobile accident. She was going back to Paris to see the child from her mother's house in Switzerland. Her mother lived in Switzerland because she had escaped from Germany and had moved to Switzerland. They had a [INAUDIBLE] passport. And so, she was killed. I was in Mexico making a film at the time, and she had a sort of dame de companion who had been her nanny when she was a little girl and stayed with her. And she hated me because I was always away. I was never there. She thought I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my-- that woman I married. And so when they sent a telegram to me, she tore it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who tore it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: This nanny, this dame de companion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell me. I found it out in the newspapers. Of course, I couldn't leave a film to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who looked after your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her uncle. He took her to Argentina, and there she has seven children. I sent her a telegram, close the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you didn't see much of your daughter as she was growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, until one day I was sitting in a restaurant in New York, and a lady came to the door and said, oh, Charles, the detectives are looking all over Europe for you. It turned out that my daughter was trying to find me with a detective. And then some friends of mine who live in Acapulco arranged for her to come down there and meet me there, this very close friend of mine, Ricky di Portanova and his wife. And his wife was wonderful for my daughter. They gave parties to make it look as if I was acceptable, to make me look good in the eyes of my daughter. Very nervous, you know, to see your daughter after all those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years didn't you see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 30 some years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a good relationship with her now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, wonderful. And April is so helpful with it. April, they love her. We've been down there, and the Argentine Air Force invited me to Antarctica. And so April came and stayed with my daughter a little bit, and they get along like a house afire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter has this wonderful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, seven children. And the six boys all play polo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now I know that you've done some other fascinating things in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Sometimes you wonder how fascinating they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have devoted a lot of your life to helping people.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I suppose when-- as I say, in school, we were taught you're supposed to, you know, but I got involved in the Congo thing a little bit, and Hungary. I worked a good bit up there with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually in these countries, or you helped from abroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I was doing, I was working with-- most of them were Knights of Malta. Later on, I became a Knight when they put me in the Knights of Malta, too, but not with that group. And we were bringing people from the frontier to, you know, tell them where to go and so forth. We spent the whole winter up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got involved in the Agadir earthquake. The-- the ambassador in Rome, the Moroccan ambassador, phoned me to say that they'd had this terrible earthquake. So I knew a general in the Air Force who had helped us when we were filming, so I woke him up and asked him if he could lend us a plane. Can you imagine some-- how stupid can you be?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a friend who was with [? squibs, ?] you know. He got a lot of medicine for us. And we had a little bit of time to wait. The general said he was going to send a plane. And I went in a hospital nearby, and when I left about an hour later, there's seven young doctors, students, came with me. So we wait at the airport, and we wait, and we wait, and just about dawn, a little dot appears. Everybody's furious at me because they think here we are here with all this equipment and these doctors, and no plane. A little dot appears, and then a few minutes later the big plane--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Agadir. And we were one of the first planes to land there. And then I became quite close friends with the crown prince, who is now the king there. But he's the most incredible man there ever was. He worked over 20 hours a day. I don't know how he did it. He was the-- I suppose is the person that I-- I would say I admire the most of anyone I know today. And another one is-- I've been involved with the Afghan thing a lot, too-- is Yaqub Khan, the foreign minister. And President Zia, I got to be very good friends with him. And I've worked a good bit with the Afghan question. That's another big passion of mine. But nothing was ever as passionate as the Jewish question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How did you meet your current wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. She's terrific. She was a student with a painter friend of mine, and she used to-- I was in the theater in Rome doing a play, and she used to come backstage, and we started going out together a lot. After 33 years together, then we-- we were going to Washington for this thing, and we decided to get married. We were married by an Afghan mullah with 65 Afghans. We asked him to come with us to Las Vegas and be our witness. We were in California at the time on the way to the Holocaust Museum opening, and he said, well, come tomorrow night and we'll talk about it. And we did, and there were 65 Afghans, and he married us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going to the opening of the Museum of Tolerance in LA or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the one in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's quite something, you know. And they have a lot of things there about Fry and all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think I-- I think I was really lucky. It was one of the big events in my life sentimentally, you know, and it's-- Then, of course, I never mentioned it after that, and nobody else ever did, and so it just sort of in the background until this Museum thing opened, and then they started contacting me and asked me to come there. And I had to make speeches and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see Fry again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not after that. And he died not long after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any contact with him until--

speaker: interviewee
text: We wrote back and forth sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew that he had passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yes. Of course, I was never in America, you see. I was always in Europe then, at that time. And I was sorry because I'd liked him and I'd admired him so much. I admired his attitude, too, very much. I mean, you know, he was modest. And I think all of us, if we had been inclined to run around saying, oh, you know, I think we would have been hesitant because we knew Fry would not have done that. And he died sort of-- and now, the newspapers are full of him. He'd been given the Legion d'Honneur from the French government, and Israel gave him the highest honor they can give. And I don't know, a lot of things. Of course, he's been dead a long time. But he wouldn't have done it for those things, you see. He didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was genuine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He-- he was-- he was pure. He didn't have any-- inspiration, you know. And even if we-- I'm sure that a lot of things we did, we would have been ashamed not to have done them, and it was because of Fry as any other reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: He inspired you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met up with some of the other people who were in the group at the opening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, at the Holocaust thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time you had contact with them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: First time, yes, first time. And we have great admiration for Beamish, you know, [? Hirschfeld, ?] Professor [? Hirschfeld. ?] And also for [INAUDIBLE] and all these--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they are all still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. And Mary Jane Gold, and Miriam Davenport [INAUDIBLE]. Very active. Mary Jane is doing a film on this subject. And the cassette that I gave, you know, about that-- that man who did that is going to do her story, and her story deals with this period, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all live in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she lives part of the time in the south of France, but most of the time in America. [BACKGROUND NOISE]
